prompt: In a state machine, what is a 'transition'?
choices: A named situation the process can rest in
A rule that moves the process from one state to another in response to an event
A copy of the entire process history kept in memory
The server that runs the workflow

prompt: What is the main purpose of checkpointing a long-running workflow?
choices: To make the workflow run faster
To save the current state so the work can survive a crash or pause and be resumed later
To delete old states that are no longer needed
To prevent users from submitting new requests

prompt: Which of the following are benefits of modeling a long-running process as a state machine with checkpoints? (Select all that apply.)
choices: The process becomes durable — it survives crashes and restarts
Work can be resumed or handed off to a different worker
Working memory stays bounded instead of holding the full history
The process can skip directly to any state, ignoring the defined transitions

prompt: A workflow defines transitions: Draft → Submitted → In Review → Approved. There is no transition directly from Draft to Approved. What does this guarantee?
choices: A request can never be approved without first being submitted and reviewed
The Draft state is automatically deleted after submission
Approved requests are stored faster than drafts
The reviewer can edit the request while it is in Draft
